title: Tholkaappiyam — 'aa' as ancient Tamil morpheme for cow
description: Ancient Tamil grammar treatise establishes that the sound form 'aa' (ஆ) for cow is ancient, long before the written script came about.
source_citation: Tholkaappiyam (ancient Tamil grammar treatise; authorship and date uncertain). Referenced in: Shanmugham P., Research Keys p.10-11.
paper_endnote: iii — Tholkaappiyam is of obscure authorship; most probably a grammar created for the spoken language, as speech predates writing by 200,000 years.

title: Purnachandra Jeeva — Tamil syllabic structure of Indus script
description: Researcher of 30+ years who first identified Tamil syllabic structure in the script. VPS2024 extends and modifies Jeeva's phonemes and morphemes, adding tally marks, modifiers, motifs.
source_citation: Jeeva, Purnachandra. sinthuveLiyil munthu thamiz (சிந்துவெளியில் முந்து தமிழ்). Yalisaip Pathippagam. ISBN 978-81-9427-9105. Published 2020. (Tamil language).
paper_endnote: iv — Current author uses some results but has deviated in many ways, both in phonemes and morphemes, adding tally marks, modifiers, motifs, and enlarged information densities.

title: RMRL Indus Script Web Tool — seal images and corpus
description: Most Indus-Harappan seals shown in figures are taken from Indus Script Web Tool, Roja Muthiah Research Library (RMRL) Chennai, India.
source_citation: Indus Script Web Tool, Roja Muthiah Research Library (RMRL), Chennai, India. www.indusscript.in. Honorary Consultant: Iravatham Mahadevan.
paper_endnote: v — Most of the Indus-Harappan seals shown in figures are taken from Indus Script Web Tool, RMRL Chennai (www.indusscript.in)

title: CISI Volume 1 — Collections in India
description: Primary corpus reference for seals from Indian excavations.
source_citation: Joshi, Jagat P. and Parpola, Asko (eds.). Corpus of Indus Seals and Inscriptions. Vol.1: Collections in India. Annales Academiae Scientiarum Fennicae, Series B, Vol.239. Helsinki, 1987.
paper_endnote: v — Some seals references without seal numbers but with motifs are taken from CISI Vol.1 (1987)

title: CISI Volume 2 — Collections in Pakistan
description: Primary corpus reference for seals from Pakistani excavations (Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Chanhu-daro).
source_citation: Shah, Sayid G.G.M. and Parpola, Asko (eds.). Corpus of Indus Seals and Inscriptions. Vol.2: Collections in Pakistan. Helsinki, 1991.

title: N. Yadav et al. — sign frequency distribution
description: Frequency distribution of most occurring signs. The 'aa'/cow sign frequency of 1395 is cited as expected in an agrarian civilisation centred on animal husbandry.
source_citation: Yadav, N. et al. Sign frequency distribution. Harappa.com. (Date unspecified in paper).
paper_endnote: ix — Frequency distribution of most occurring signs: Credit Dr. N. Yadav, et all. From Harappa.com

title: Rao et al. (2009) — entropic evidence for linguistic structure
description: Entropy analysis shows Indus signs have conditional entropy consistent with natural languages, not random or simple emblematic systems. Provides statistical foundation that signs encode language.
source_citation: Rao, R.P.N. et al. Entropic Evidence for Linguistic Structure in the Indus Script. Science 324:1165. 2009.
paper_endnote: Cited as supporting linguistic structure of script (not attributed to specific endnote in paper)

title: Rajan & Sivamantham (2025) — Indus signs and Tamil Nadu graffiti marks
description: Morphological study showing continuity between Indus signs and graffiti marks on pottery in Tamil Nadu. Supports Tamil linguistic substrate hypothesis.
source_citation: Rajan, K. and Sivamantham, R. Indus Signs and Graffiti Marks of Tamil Nadu, a morphological study. Published by Department of Archaeology, Government of Tamil Nadu. 2025.
paper_endnote: viii — Indus Signs and Graffiti Marks of Tamil Nadu, K.Rajan, R Sivamantham, Dept of Archaeology, Govt of Tamil Nadu, 2025

title: Farmer, Sproat, Witzel (2004) — non-linguistic emblem thesis (opposing)
description: Primary opposing hypothesis. Argues brevity and singleton rate indicate signs are non-linguistic. VPS2024 rebuts: brevity is a function of purpose as identifiers/permits; singletons explained by modifier system; long-form writing on perishable materials.
source_citation: Farmer, S., Sproat, R., Witzel, M. The Collapse of the Indus Script Thesis: The myth of a literate Harappan civilization. Electronic Journal of Vedic Studies. 02/11/2004.
paper_endnote: vii — well known in their field
